text:
Functions forms tissues. Different tissues are organised to form an organ and several organs work together to form organ systems. Such as, nasal pores, nasal cavity, trachea and lungs form respiratory system.

Even when cells are organised into tissues, organs, and organ systems the cell remains the fundamental unit of structure and function in all living organisms. This makes us wonder how such tiny cells perform so many different activities. What are the different components of a cell? How do cells live, communicate with each other? Do cells live forever, or do they die? In this chapter, we will explore the answers to these questions as we enter the fascinating world of cells!

What do we call the ability of the human eye to see two very close objects as separate and distinct? Imagine two tiny dots drawn on a piece of paper. As the dots are moved closer, they come to a point at which they can no longer be seen as separate. When viewed from about 25 cm (the near point of human eye), two points separated by about 0.1 mm can be seen as separate. When they appear as a single point. This is called the limit of resolution of the human eye, which is 0.1 mm.

A cell is usually too small, to be seen by the unaided eye (Fig. 2.1). This raises an important question—how have cell biologists studied the structure and function of cells that are much smaller than the limit of resolution of the human eye?

You have learnt about a convex lens. A convex lens or a combination of lenses, i.e., an objective lens and an eyepiece are used for the magnification of an object (Fig. 2.1) to make it appear larger.

Robert Hooke was the first person to observe a cell in 1665 using a self-designed microscope (capable of about 200–300X magnification). While examining a thin slice of cork, he observed small box-like compartments and named them 'cells'. In school laboratories, light microscopes are used to observe objects using

diagrams.description: This diagram is a detailed, vertical cross-section illustrating the process of observing cells using increasing levels of magnification, ranging from the unaided human eye to high-powered microscopes. The diagram is structured vertically, with the magnification scale running along the left side (from 1x to 1000x). At the top, the human eye is shown, indicating the limit of resolution (0.1 mm). Below this, the diagram shows the components of a compound microscope: an objective lens and an eyepiece. The diagram then illustrates the increasing visibility of structures as magnification increases. At the 100x level, a plant cell and an animal cell are visible. At the 1000x level, various microscopic life forms are shown, including bacteria, and the internal structures of cells are detailed. The diagram uses color to differentiate structures, showing the relative sizes of the observed objects (e.g., the large size of the plant cell compared to the small size of the bacteria).

captions: Fig. 2.1: Size of the objects and its visibility through unaided to aided eye
